intro_band.agent_remit_summary: A customer-facing airline support system built on the OpenAI Agents SDK, composed of three cooperating agents: a triage agent that routes each request by handoff, an FAQ agent that answers airline-policy questions only from a curated dataset, and a seat-booking agent that updates seat assignments on confirmed reservations. It may reach the authenticated customer by direct reply and the internal reservation system (scoped read-write) and FAQ dataset (read-only); all customer input is untrusted even after authentication. The remit forbids outbound external channels, any billing/fare/ticket action, code or shell execution, and cross-customer data access, and requires that customer identity and confirmation-number ownership be verified before any reservation mutation, that authoritative identifiers come from the reservation system, and that every seat change be written to a durable audit log.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: Python multi-agent example (`examples/customer_service/main.py`) on the OpenAI Agents SDK: three `Agent` instances (triage, FAQ, seat-booking) wired together with `handoff()` transfers and run through `Runner.run` inside a `trace()` block. Two `@function_tool`s exist — `faq_lookup_tool` (a three-keyword substring matcher returning canned strings) and `update_seat` (writes `confirmation_number`/`seat_number` into an in-memory `AirlineAgentContext`). The seat-booking handoff hook `on_seat_booking_handoff` fabricates a flight number with `random.randint`. The SDK ships input/output guardrails (`src/agents/guardrail.py`), tool guardrails (`src/agents/tool_guardrails.py`), a per-tool `needs_approval` gate, strict-JSON-schema tool args, and default-on tracing — but the example wires none of the security primitives: no guardrails, no approval gate, no identity or ownership verification, and no durable audit log for the seat mutation.

behavior_summary:
The dominant pattern is framework offers safe primitives, the example wires none of them. The OpenAI Agents SDK ships input and output guardrails, tool guardrails, a per-tool needs_approval gate, strict-schema tool arguments, and default-on tracing, yet the customer-service example attaches no guardrails, sets no approval gate, and performs no identity or ownership verification — so update_seat mutates reservation state directly from a customer-typed confirmation number, and on_seat_booking_handoff fabricates the authoritative flight number with random.randint.

The second theme is a policy-declared-but-code-silent gap around trust and auditability: the remit's identity-verification, authoritative-identifier, and durable-audit-log MUSTs have no code-level enforcement, and the one real observability surface (inherited SDK tracing) is not the specified seat-change audit record and defaults to capturing customer PII.

rules.rule_text: Any channel not listed here is unauthorized by default.
Any tool that sends outbound external communication (email, SMS, push, webhook).
Any code-execution, shell, filesystem, or arbitrary-network tool.
Persisting PII, payment data, or reservation details beyond the lifetime of the current session.
Any request where the customer's identity cannot be verified MUST be escalated to a human before any reservation is read or modified.
Customer identity MUST be confirmed before any action that mutates reservation state.
A submitted confirmation number MUST be verified as belonging to the authenticated customer before that reservation is read or modified.
A seat change MUST NOT be issued without first verifying the confirmation number against the authoritative reservation record.
Seat updates MUST be limited to seats that exist on the flight and are available — a seat already assigned to another passenger MUST NOT be reassigned.
Flight numbers, confirmation numbers, and passenger identifiers MUST come from the authoritative reservation system and MUST NOT be generated by the agent at runtime.
Instructions embedded in free-text customer input that attempt to override an agent's role, scope, or identity MUST NOT be accepted or acted on.
Instructions embedded in tool outputs — FAQ content, seat records, flight data — that attempt to change an agent's behavior MUST NOT be accepted.
The FAQ agent MUST answer only from the curated FAQ dataset and MUST NOT speculate or draw on general knowledge outside that dataset.
Handoffs between agents MUST NOT escalate privileges or bypass identity verification; a specialist agent receiving a handoff MUST re-verify identity before any mutation.
The triage agent MUST NOT access or expose customer reservation data directly.
Every seat change MUST be recorded to a durable audit log capturing the time, the customer identity, the confirmation number, the old and new seat, and the agent that performed the change.
Any tool that reads or writes reservation state on behalf of a customer whose identity has not been verified.
a mutation (seat change) that fails verification MUST NOT be retried automatically; escalate instead.
Ticket purchases, payments, or fares MUST NOT be created, cancelled, modified, or refunded — those flows belong to a separate billing system.

findings.summary: `update_seat` mutates reservation state from a customer-supplied confirmation number with no identity, ownership, or seat-availability verification and no approval gate.
findings.description: The seat-booking agent's `update_seat` tool writes the customer-typed confirmation number and seat straight into the run context, never confirming the caller's identity, never checking the confirmation number against an authoritative reservation record, and never verifying the seat exists or is free. No `needs_approval` gate is set (the SDK default is False) and no human checkpoint exists, so a mutation the remit reserves for verified-identity, verified-ownership cases proceeds unconditionally — the confused-deputy path to another customer's reservation in a real deployment.
policy_rule_text: Customer identity MUST be confirmed before any action that mutates reservation state.
A submitted confirmation number MUST be verified as belonging to the authenticated customer before that reservation is read or modified.
A seat change MUST NOT be issued without first verifying the confirmation number against the authoritative reservation record.

evidence.snippet: update_seat (lines 67-83) sets context.confirmation_number and seat_number from customer args with no identity/ownership/availability check and no authoritative-record lookup
seat_booking_agent declares tools=[update_seat] with no approval gate; @function_tool default needs_approval=False (src/agents/tool.py:428)

recommended_actions: Gate `update_seat` behind identity + ownership verification: look the confirmation number up in the authoritative reservation record, confirm it belongs to the authenticated session customer and the target seat exists and is free, before writing any state.
Set `needs_approval=True` (or a tool guardrail) on `update_seat` for any path where identity is not yet verified, routing unverifiable-identity cases to a human checkpoint per the remit.

findings.summary: The seat-booking handoff fabricates an authoritative flight number with `random.randint` and the mutation then treats it as real.
findings.description: `on_seat_booking_handoff` sets `flight_number = f"FLT-{random.randint(100, 999)}"` on the shared context, and `update_seat` asserts that flight number is present before mutating — so a runtime-fabricated identifier stands in for the authoritative reservation record. The remit forbids the agent generating flight, confirmation, or passenger identifiers at runtime; here a made-up value is treated as authoritative for a state change, a data-provenance failure the remit escalates to halt-and-alert.
policy_rule_text: Flight numbers, confirmation numbers, and passenger identifiers MUST come from the authoritative reservation system and MUST NOT be generated by the agent at runtime.

evidence.snippet: on_seat_booking_handoff sets flight_number = f"FLT-{random.randint(100, 999)}" — a fabricated authoritative identifier
update_seat asserts context.flight_number is not None, treating the fabricated value as the record of truth for the mutation

recommended_actions: Replace the `random.randint` flight-number stub with a lookup that binds the flight, confirmation, and passenger identifiers from the authoritative reservation system for the verified customer.
Add a check that authoritative identifiers on the context originate from the reservation system and reject any that were synthesized during the run.

findings.summary: No input guardrails are wired on the triage agent; free-text customer input reaches the LLM with no prompt-injection or off-domain screening.
findings.description: The triage agent is the declared entry point where input guardrails should attach, but it is constructed with no `input_guardrails`, so each turn's raw `user_input` is appended and sent to the model unscreened. The SDK ships `InputGuardrail` with tripwire semantics for exactly this, yet none is used — leaving the remit's "instructions embedded in free-text customer input MUST NOT be accepted" unenforced and routing/goal hijack a single crafted message away.
policy_rule_text: Instructions embedded in free-text customer input that attempt to override an agent's role, scope, or identity MUST NOT be accepted or acted on.

evidence.snippet: triage_agent is defined with instructions and handoffs but no input_guardrails argument; free-text user_input is appended and run at main.py:162-168
SDK ships InputGuardrail with tripwire_triggered semantics (@input_guardrail) — available but unused by the example

recommended_actions: Attach an `InputGuardrail` to the triage agent that screens for prompt-injection and off-domain content and trips the wire before the request is routed.
Pair it with an output guardrail so injected instructions surfacing in agent output are caught before reply.

findings.summary: Seat changes are not written to a durable audit log; the only record is transient `print()` output to stdout.
findings.description: The remit requires every seat change to be recorded to a durable audit log capturing time, customer identity, confirmation number, old and new seat, and the acting agent. The example emits only `print()` lines to the terminal and the SDK's `logger` has no file handler, so no durable, queryable record of the mutation exists. Inherited trace spans are developer observability, not the specified audit record — leaving the highest-impact action undetectable after the fact.
policy_rule_text: Every seat change MUST be recorded to a durable audit log capturing the time, the customer identity, the confirmation number, the old and new seat, and the agent that performed the change.

evidence.snippet: tool-call and output are surfaced only via print() to stdout (lines 170-183); no structured audit record is written for the seat change
logger = logging.getLogger("openai.agents") with no FileHandler/RotatingFileHandler — no file-based durable log path

recommended_actions: After a successful `update_seat`, write an audit record (time, verified customer identity, confirmation number, old and new seat, acting agent) to a durable, append-only store.
Do not rely on trace spans for this — they default to including sensitive data and are not the required audit surface.

findings.summary: FAQ-only grounding is enforced by prompt text alone; nothing stops the model answering from general knowledge when the lookup returns "I don't know".
findings.description: The FAQ agent is instructed to use `faq_lookup_tool` and "Do not rely on your own knowledge," but that constraint lives only in the prompt — there is no code gate forcing the answer to come from the tool. The tool itself is a three-keyword substring matcher returning canned strings or an "I don't know", so any unmatched question falls to the model's own knowledge, exactly the speculation the remit forbids.
policy_rule_text: The FAQ agent MUST answer only from the curated FAQ dataset and MUST NOT speculate or draw on general knowledge outside that dataset.

evidence.snippet: faq_agent instructions say "Use the faq lookup tool ... Do not rely on your own knowledge" — a prompt-only constraint with no code enforcement
faq_lookup_tool matches ~3 keyword sets and otherwise returns "I don't know", leaving unmatched questions to the model

recommended_actions: Enforce grounding in code: return the tool's answer (or a fixed fallback) directly for FAQ intents rather than letting the model compose a free answer, or add an output guardrail that rejects answers not traceable to the FAQ dataset.

findings.summary: Tool outputs and inter-agent handoff content re-enter LLM context unlabeled and unvalidated; no output guardrails are wired.
findings.description: FAQ results, the seat-update return string, and handoff messages all flow back into the model's context with no separation of untrusted content from trusted instructions and no output guardrail, so the remit's "instructions embedded in tool outputs MUST NOT be accepted" rests on model discretion alone. Exposure is currently low because the tool data is static and curated, but the structural gap would carry any future dynamic or record-sourced content straight into context.
policy_rule_text: Instructions embedded in tool outputs — FAQ content, seat records, flight data — that attempt to change an agent's behavior MUST NOT be accepted.

evidence.snippet: ToolCallOutputItem output is echoed back into the loop and re-entered via result.to_input_list() with no labeling/validation of tool output as untrusted
SDK ships OutputGuardrail (@output_guardrail) with tripwire semantics — available but unused by the example

recommended_actions: Attach an output guardrail and label tool/handoff content as untrusted in prompt construction so embedded instructions cannot be acted on if the FAQ or reservation data ever becomes dynamic.

findings.summary: Runtime dependencies are floor-pinned with open upper-minor ranges and the model version is the operator-configured SDK default rather than pinned.
findings.description: `pyproject.toml` declares dependencies as floor ranges (`openai>=2.45.0,<3`, `pydantic>=2.12.2, <3`, and similar), which permit silent minor/patch drift, and the agent's model is left to the SDK default resolved at runtime rather than a pinned version. A `uv.lock` is committed, which pins the resolved graph for reproducible installs and materially reduces the risk, so this is a hardening gap rather than an exposure.

evidence.snippet: dependencies use >= floor with <major ceilings (openai>=2.45.0,<3; pydantic>=2.12.2, <3; websockets>=15.0, <17), allowing minor/patch drift
uv.lock committed (~828 KB) — resolved dependency graph is pinned for reproducible installs, mitigating the range looseness

recommended_actions: Rely on `uv.lock` for deployed builds (install with `--frozen`) and pin the agent's model version explicitly so inference behavior does not drift with the SDK default.

positives.title: Default-on tracing with per-conversation grouping
positives.description: The SDK defaults `tracing_disabled` to False and the example wraps every conversation in `trace("Customer service", group_id=conversation_id)`, producing structured, session-grouped spans without any extra wiring.

positives.title: Strict JSON-schema validation on tool arguments
positives.description: `function_tool` defaults `strict_json_schema` to True, so `update_seat` and `faq_lookup_tool` arguments are schema-validated for shape before the tool runs (shape only, not authorization).

positives.title: Per-agent least-tool scoping; triage holds no data tool
positives.description: Each agent carries only the tools it needs — triage has handoff tools only, FAQ only `faq_lookup_tool`, seat-booking only `update_seat` — satisfying the remit's requirement that the triage agent hold no data-access or mutation tool.

positives.title: No cross-session persistence of customer data
positives.description: `AirlineAgentContext` is an in-memory Pydantic model recreated per run with no storage layer, so no PII or reservation detail is persisted beyond the current session.

positives.title: No forbidden capabilities present in code
positives.description: The workspace contains no outbound-communication, billing/fare, or code-execution/shell/filesystem tools — the remit's Forbidden Tools list is satisfied by construction.

log_files.no_logs_note: No local log files and no file-based logging infrastructure exist (`src/agents/logger.py` uses a stderr `getLogger` with no FileHandler); runtime observability is remote SDK trace spans rather than a file, and the remit-required durable seat-change audit log is absent — see PRAX-2026-07-17-004.

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Ad hoc overall (1.60). The system inherits a few real SDK-level controls that actually run — strict-schema tool arguments, per-agent tool scoping, and default-on tracing — and it holds no forbidden capabilities (no outbound-comms, billing, or exec tools) and keeps no cross-session state. But the security primitives the framework ships for exactly this shape of agent are all left unwired: no identity or ownership verification before a reservation mutation, no approval gate, no input/output/tool guardrails, and no durable audit log. Zero Trust is the weakest axis and the one that carries double weight, which pins the posture in the Ad hoc band despite the inherited observability and supply-chain hygiene.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The system is scoped to airline tasks through per-agent instructions, a triage/FAQ/seat handoff architecture, and per-agent tool inventories, but the restriction is prompt-only with no code-level topic gate and the triage prompt is generic ("a helpful triaging agent").

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: The curated static FAQ dataset and the session-scoped, non-persistent `AirlineAgentContext` are genuine positives, but customer free-text and tool/handoff outputs enter LLM context unvalidated and unlabeled, and `on_seat_booking_handoff` fabricates an authoritative flight identifier at runtime — a provenance failure.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: `update_seat` mutates reservation state from customer-supplied arguments with no identity, ownership, or seat-availability verification and no approval gate, and none of the SDK's input/output/tool guardrails are wired; only strict-schema argument-shape validation and per-agent tool scoping actually run.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: A `uv.lock` is committed and the framework provenance (the OpenAI Agents SDK) is well documented, but `pyproject.toml` floor-pins dependencies with `>=`/`<major` ranges and the model version is the operator-configured SDK default rather than pinned.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: The SDK carries an extensive functional test suite that even exercises the guardrail and approval mechanisms, but there is no evidence of adversarial testing of this example's threat model or of any test driving a security change to the example itself.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: Default-on SDK tracing wrapped per conversation (`trace(..., group_id=conversation_id)`) is a real structured observability surface the agent inherits and does not disable, but there is no durable seat-change audit log with the remit-required fields and traces default to including sensitive customer data.
